entry.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.6 A Unified and Systemic Lens on Diffusion Models

link.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.6 A Unified and Systemic Lens on Diffusion Models

entry.content:
Diffusion Model Conceptual Reconstruction following The Principles of Diffusion Models


Hozy Summary



Concept) Four Prediction Types

  • Types)
    • $\epsilon$-Prediction
    • $\mathbf{x}$-Prediction
    • Score-Prediction
    • $\mathbf{v}$-Prediction


Variational View Score-Based View Flow-Based View
Intractable original training objective $$\mathcal{J}_{ ext{KL}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)} \left[ \mathcal{D}_{ ext{KL}}(p(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t}\mid\mathbf{x}_t)) \;\Vert\; p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t}\mid\mathbf{x}_t) ight]$$ $$\mathcal{J}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)} \left[ \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) \Vert_2^2 ight]$$ $$\mathcal{J}_{ ext{FM}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)} \left[ \Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t) \Vert_2^2 ight]$$
Tractable objectives by conditioning on the data $$\mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{ ext{data}}$$ $$\mathcal{J}_{ ext{KL}}(\phi) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0} \mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)} \left[ \mathcal{D}_{ ext{KL}}(p(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t}\mid\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)) \;\Vert\; p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t}\mid\mathbf{x}_t) ight]}_{\mathcal{J}_{ ext{KL}}(\phi) ext{ (Conditional KL)}} + C$$ $$\mathcal{J}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0} \mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)} \left[ \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_0) \Vert_2^2 ight]}_{\mathcal{J}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi)} + C$$ $$\mathcal{J}_{ ext{FM}}(\phi) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0} \mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)} \left[ \Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_0) \Vert_2^2 ight]}_{\mathcal{J}_{ ext{CFM}}(\phi)} + C$$
Common forward perturbation kernel $$p_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathcal{N} igg( \mathbf{x}_t;\; \alpha_t\mathbf{x}_0, \sigma_t^2\mathbf{I} igg)$$
Parameterization Target $\epsilon$-Prediction $$\epsilon_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \approx \mathbb{E}[\epsilon\mid\mathbf{x}_t] = \epsilon^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t)$$ $\mathbf{x}$-Prediction $$\mathbf{x}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \approx \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}_0\mid\mathbf{x}_t] = \mathbf{x}^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t)$$ Score-Prediction $$ egin{aligned} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) &\approx abla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) \ &= \mathbb{E}[ abla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_0)\mid\mathbf{x}_t] \ &= s^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \end{aligned}$$ $\mathbf{v}$-Prediction $$\mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \approx \mathbb{E}\left[ rac{ ext{d}\mathbf{x}_t}{ ext{d}t}\mid\mathbf{x}_t ight] = \mathbf{v}^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t)$$
Training objective $$\mathcal{L}_{ ext{noise}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_t\left[ \omega(t)\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0, \epsilon}\Vert \epsilon_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \epsilon \Vert_2^2 ight]$$ $$\mathcal{L}_{ ext{clean}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_t\left[ \omega(t)\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0, \epsilon}\Vert \mathbf{x}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{x}_0 \Vert_2^2 ight]$$ $$\mathcal{L}_{ ext{score}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_t\left[ \omega(t)\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0, \epsilon}\Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - abla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_0) \Vert_2^2 ight]$$ $$\mathcal{L}_{ ext{velocity}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_t\left[ \omega(t)\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0, \epsilon}\Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_0,\epsilon) \Vert_2^2 ight]$$
Conditional Parameterization Target $$A_t\mathbf{x}_0+B_t\epsilon$$ $\epsilon$ $\mathbf{x}_0$ $$-\displaystyle rac{1}{\sigma_t}\epsilon$$ $$\alpha_t'\mathbf{x}_0 + \sigma_t'\epsilon$$



Prop.) Equivalence of the Four Parameterizations



Prop.) PF-ODE in each Parameterization

  • cf.) Although the four parameterizations are equivalent in principle, they differ in practice.
    • Why?)
      • Each parametrization changes has its own
        • the stiffness of the vector field
        • the behavior of discretization error
        • the ease of optimization
    • For the fast sampling with advanced ODE solvers, $\epsilon$- or $\mathbf{x}$-prediction is preferred.
      • Why?) They align well with the solver inputs and reduce error accumulation.
    • For the limited number of function evaluations, $\mathbf{x}$- or $\mathbf{v}$-prediction is preferred.
      • Why?) They often yield smoother objectives and better step to step consistency.



Prop.) All Affine Flows are Equivalent

  • Settings)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t^{ ext{FM}} = (1-t)\mathbf{x}_0 + t\epsilon = \mathbf{x}_0 + t\underbrace{(\epsilon - \mathbf{x}_0)}_{\mathbf{v}}\).
      • i.e.) A canonical interpolation used in CFM and RF.



Prop.) Conversions between the Parameterizations

  • Settings)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t = \alpha_t\mathbf{x}_0 + \sigma_t\epsilon\) s.t.
      • \(\sigma_t\gt0\).
      • \((\alpha_t'\sigma_t - \alpha_t\sigma_t') e0\).
    • Oracle targets are given by
      • \(\epsilon^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t) = \mathbb{E}[\epsilon\mid\mathbf{x}_t]\).
      • \(\mathbf{x}_0^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}_0\mid\mathbf{x}_t]\).
      • \(\mathbf{v}^*(\mathbf{x}_t, t) = \mathbb{E}[\alpha_t'\mathbf{x}_0 + \sigma_t' \epsilon\mid\mathbf{x}_t]\).
  • Conversions
    • Score - Noise
      • Parameterization
        • \(s_\phi \equiv - rac{1}{\sigma_t}\epsilon_\phi\).
      • Loss
        • \(\left\Vert s_\phi - abla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) ight\Vert_2^2 = rac{1}{\sigma_t^2} \left\Vert \epsilon_{\phi} - \epsilon^* ight\Vert_2^2\).
    • Score - $\mathbf{x}$
      • Parameterization
        • \(s_\phi \equiv rac{\alpha_t}{\sigma_t^2}\left(\mathbf{x}_\phi - rac{\mathbf{x}_t}{\alpha_t} ight)\).
      • Loss
        • \(\left\Vert s_\phi - abla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) ight\Vert_2^2 = rac{\alpha_t^2}{\sigma_t^4} \left\Vert \mathbf{x}_\phi - \mathbf{x}_0^* ight\Vert_2^2\).
    • Score - $\mathbf{v}$
      • Parameterization
        • \(s_\phi = rac{\alpha_t}{\sigma_t(\alpha_t'\sigma_t - \alpha_t\sigma_t')}\mathbf{v}_\phi - rac{\alpha_t'}{\sigma_t(\alpha_t'\sigma_t - \alpha_t\sigma_t')}\mathbf{x}_t\).
      • Loss
        • \(\left\Vert s_\phi - abla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) ight\Vert_2^2 = \left( rac{\alpha_t}{\sigma_t(\alpha_t'\sigma_t - \alpha_t\sigma_t')} ight) \left\Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi - \mathbf{v}^* ight\Vert_2^2\).



Prop.) Unified perspective connecting variational, SDE, and ODE formulations through the continuity equation, where all p_t(x) evolve under a shared dynamic.

entry.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.5 Flow-Based Perspective - From NFs to Flow Matching

link.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.5 Flow-Based Perspective - From NFs to Flow Matching

entry.content:
Diffusion Model Conceptual Reconstruction following The Principles of Diffusion Models


Hozy Summary

  • Normalizing Flows (NF)
    • Idea)
      • Use sequence of invertible transformation from $p_{ ext{data}}$ to $p_{ ext{prior}}$.
        • cf.) Change-of-Variables Formula : \(p(\mathbf{x}) = p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{z})\displaystyle\left\vert ext{det} rac{\partial \mathbf{f}^-1(\mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} ight\vert,\quad ext{where } \mathbf{z}=\mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})\).
    • Loss)
      \( egin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{ ext{NF}}(\phi) &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}}\left[\log_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight] \ &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}}\left[ \log p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{z}) + \log\left\vert ext{det} rac{\partial f_\phi^{-1}(\mathbf{x})}{\partial\mathbf{x}} ight\vert ight] \ \end{aligned}\).
    • Sampling)
      • Draw \(\mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{ ext{prior}}\).
      • Compute \(\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_0)\).
    • Limit)
      • Limited choice of transformation.
      • Trade off between the expressiveness of the model and the computational cost.
        • Must calculate the determinant of the Jacobian $O(D^3)$
  • Neural ODE (NODE)
    • Idea)
      • Continuous transformation adopted to the NFs
        • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}\mathbf{x}_{t}}{ ext{d}t} = \mathbf{v}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t),\quad t\in[0,T]\).
    • Loss)
      • MLE of \(\mathcal{L}_ ext{NODE}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_ ext{data}}\left[ \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x},T) ight]\)
    • Sampling)
      • Draw \(\mathbf{x}(0)\sim p_{ ext{prior}}\).
      • Compute \(\displaystyle\mathbf{x}(T) = \mathbf{x}(0) + \int_0^T \mathbf{v}_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) ext{d}t\).
    • Limit)
      • ODE solver is expensive due to the integration.
  • Flow Matching
    • Idea)
      • Relationship between \(\mathbf{x}_t,\;p_t,\;\Psi_{0 ightarrow t} ext{, and }\mathbf{v}_t\)
        • \(\mathbf{x}_t\sim p_t\).
        • \(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}_t\).
        • \( rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t}\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x})\).
      • \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x})\)-prediction
        • i.e.) Parameterize the velocity field, i.e. \(\mathbf{v}_\phi \approx \mathbf{v}_t\).
        • Similarity with the NODE.
      • Since \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x})\) is intractable, use conditional density, conditional flow, and conditional velocity field instead
        • i.e.) \(\mathbf{v}_\phi(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z}) \approx \mathbf{v}_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})\).
    • Training)
      • Loss)
        • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{CFM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{t,\mathbf{x}\sim\pi(\mathbf{z}),\mathbf{x}_t\sim p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})}\left[ \Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{z}) \Vert^2 ight] + C\).
      • Implementation)
        • Obtain \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{z})\) by…
          • setting $p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$ (conditional probability path) first : Eulerian View
          • setting $\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$ (conditional flow) first : Lagrangian View
        • We may choose the latent $\mathbf{z}$ by…
          1. Two-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = (\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)\)
          2. One-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_0\) or \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_1\)



Prop.) Change-of-Variables Formula of Densities

  • Prop.)
    • For
      • $\mathbf{f}$ : an invertible transformation
      • $\mathbf{z}\sim p_{ ext{prior}}$
    • the density of $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z})$ is
      • \(p(\mathbf{x}) = p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{z})\displaystyle\left\vert ext{det} rac{\partial \mathbf{f}^-1(\mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} ight\vert,\quad ext{where } \mathbf{z}=\mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})\).
Intuition hozy note


5.1 Flow-Based Models: Normalizing Flows and Neural ODEs

Model) Normalizing Flows

Rezende and Mohamed, 2015
hozy note

  • Def.)
    • For
      • \(p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x})\) : a complex data distribution
      • \(p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{z})\) : a simple prior
      • \(\mathbf{f}_\phi:\mathbb{R}^D ightarrow\mathbb{R}^D\) with
        • \(\mathbf{x} = f_\phi(\mathbf{z})\) and \(\mathbf{z}\sim p_{ ext{prior}}\)
    • Using the change-of-variables formula, we may get the model likelihood of
      • \(\log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \displaystyle\log p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{z}) + \log\left\vert ext{det} rac{\partial f_\phi^{-1}(\mathbf{x})}{\partial\mathbf{x}} ight\vert\).
  • Training Objective)
    • We may learn parameters $\phi$ by maximizing the log-likelihood, which is equivalent to minimizing
      • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{NF}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}}\left[\log_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight]\).
  • Limit)
    • Simple linear transformation lacks expressiveness
      • Sol.) A sequence of $K$ trainable invertible mappings \(\{\mathbf{f}_k\}_{k=0}^{L-1}\)
        • Settings)
          • \(\mathbf{f}_\phi = \mathbf{f}_{L-1}\circ\mathbf{f}_{L-2}\circ\cdots\circ\mathbf{f}_{0}\) where each \(\mathbf{f}_{k}\) is parameterized by a neural network
          • \(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{f}_{k}(\mathbf{x}_{k}),\quad k=0,\ldots,L-1, \mathbf{z}=\mathbf{x}_{0}\sim p_{ ext{prior}}, \mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x}_{L}\).
        • Then we get the log-likelihood of
          • \(\log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \log p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{x}_{0}) + \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{L-1}\log\left\vert ext{det} rac{\partial \mathbf{f}_{k}}{\partial \mathbf{x}_{k}} ight\vert^{-1}\).
    • Optimizing the above loss takes \(O(D^3)\) runtime due to computing the Jacobian determinant.
      • Sol.) Using the Planar Flows or Residual Flows
  • Sampling)
    • Draw \(\mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{ ext{prior}}\).
    • Compute \(\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_0)\).


Concept) Residual Flow



Model) Neural ODEs

Chen et al., 2018

  • Idea)
    • Recall the discrete sequence of invertible mappings of Normalizing Flows of \(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{f}_{k}(\mathbf{x}_{k})\).
    • Using the parameterized velocity field, we may reconstruct it as
      • \(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} + \mathbf{v}_{\phi_k}(\mathbf{x}_{k}, k)\).
    • This formulation corresponds to the Euler discretization of the continuous time ODE of \( rac{ ext{d}\mathbf{x}_{t}}{ ext{d}t} = \mathbf{v}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t)\).
    • In the limit of infinite layers and vanishing step size ($\Delta t ightarrow0$), the discrete NF converges to a continuous model : Nueral ODE
  • Model)
    • A continuous transformation
      • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}\mathbf{x}_{t}}{ ext{d}t} = \mathbf{v}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t),\quad t\in[0,T]\).
        • where
          • \(\mathbf{x}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^D\) : the state at time $t$
          • \(\mathbf{v}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t)\) : a neural network parameterized by $\phi$
    • Using the Instantaneous Change-of-Variables Formula below, we may get the log density of $x_T$ by the neural ODE given by
      • \(\displaystyle \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{T}, T) = \log p_{ ext{prior}}(\mathbf{x}_{0}, 0) -\int_0^T abla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathbf{v}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) ext{d}t\).
  • Training)
    • Goal) \(p_\phi(\cdot, T) \approx p_{ ext{data}}\)
    • Loss)
      • MLE of \(\mathcal{L}_ ext{NODE}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_ ext{data}}\left[ \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x},T) ight]\)
    • Limit)
      • ODE solver is expensive due to the integration.
      • The adjoint sensitivity method computes gradients via an auxiliary ODE with $O(1)$ memory complexity.
  • Sampling)
    • Draw \(\mathbf{x}(0)\sim p_{ ext{prior}}\).
    • Compute \(\displaystyle\mathbf{x}(T) = \mathbf{x}(0) + \int_0^T \mathbf{v}_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) ext{d}t\).


Concept) Instantaneous Change-of-Variables Formula

  • Thm.)
    • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t} \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) = - abla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathbf{v}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t)\).
  • cf.)
    • A continuous version of Change-of-Variables Formula
    • Chen et al., 2018
    • A special case of the Fokker-Planck equation
      • Continuity Equation


Desc.) Physical Interpretation of the Continuity Equation

From Appendix B



5.2. Flow Matching Framework

  • Settings)
    • \(\{p_t\}_{t\in[0,1]}\) : a predefined probability path s.t.
      • Boundary settings
        • \(p_0 = p_{ ext{src}},\quad p_1 = p_{ ext{tgt}}\).
      • Marginal Density
        • For a latent variable $\mathbf{z}\sim\pi(\mathbf{z})$ drawn from an unknown distribution
          • Here, the common choices for $\mathbf{z}$ are
            • Two-sided conditioning : \(\mathbf{z}=(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)\sim p_{ ext{src}}(\mathbf{x}_0) p_{ ext{tgt}}(\mathbf{x}_1)\)
            • One-sided conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_0\) or \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_1\)
        • we may denote the marginal density $p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$ as
          • \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) = \displaystyle\int p_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{z})\pi(\mathbf{z}) ext{d}\mathbf{z}\) with $(\pi(\mathbf{z}), {p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})})$ chosen to satisfy the boundary conditions.
    • \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t)\) : a time-dependent vector(velocity) field whose associated ODE flow matches \(\{p_t\}_{t\in[0,1]}\)
      • s.t. induced ODE enables a sample-wise transformation of
        • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}\mathbf{x}_{t}}{ ext{d}t} = \mathbf{v}_{t}(\mathbf{x}(t)),\quad t\in[0,1]\).
        • \(\mathbf{x}(t)\sim p_t\).
      • or, equivalently captured with the Continuity Equation of
        • \(\displaystyle rac{\partial p_t(\mathbf{x})}{\partial t} + abla\cdot(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x})p_t(\mathbf{x})) = 0\).
          • cf.) For the intuition for the Continuity Equation, refer to the desc. below.
      • Any \(\mathbf{v}_t\) that satisfies the above Continuity Equation is allowed!
  • Training)
    • $\mathbf{v}$-prediction
      • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{FM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{t,\mathbf{x}_t\sim p_t}\left[ \Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t) \Vert^2 ight]\).
    • However, $\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t, t)$ is intractable.
      • Just like DDPM and DSM did, we may utilize the conditional velocity field of $\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{z})$ as follows:
        \( egin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{ ext{FM}}(\phi) &= \mathbb{E}_{t,\mathbf{x}\sim\pi(\mathbf{z}),\mathbf{x}_t\sim p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})}\left[ \Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{z}) \Vert^2 ight] + C \ & riangleq \mathcal{L}_{ ext{CFM}}(\phi) + C \ \end{aligned}\)
    • To obtain \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{z})\), we may choose either
      • to set $p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$ (conditional probability path) first : Eulerian View
      • to set $\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$ (conditional flow) first : Lagrangian View


Concept) Eulerian View

  • Goal)
    • Construct $p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$ first and then derive the corresponding $\mathbf{v}_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$.
  • E.g.) Both source and target distribution are Gaussian.
    • Procedure)
      1. Identify the marginal density \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)\).
      2. Choose a marginal flow map \(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x})\).
      3. Derive the marginal velocity field \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x})\) from \(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x})\).
      4. Show that the closed-form characterization on the marginal is valid on the conditional as well.
        • Two strategies for choosing the $\mathbf{z}$ from $p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$
          1. Two-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = (\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)\)
          2. One-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_0\) or \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_1\)
    • Derivation)
      1. Identifying the marginal \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)\).
        • Boundary conditions
          • \(p_{ ext{src}} = p_0 = \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x};\;\mu(0),\sigma^2(0)\mathbf{I} ight)\).
          • \(p_{ ext{tgt}} = p_1 = \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x};\;\mu(1),\sigma^2(1)\mathbf{I} ight)\).
        • Then the marginal density can be denoted as the interpolation of
          • \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_t;\;\mu(t),\sigma^2(t)\mathbf{I} ight)\).
          • And, \(\{p_t\}_{t\in[0,1]}\) connects \(p_{ ext{src}}\) and \(p_{ ext{tgt}}\).
      2. Choosing the marginal flow map \(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x})\).
        • There are many velocity fields that induce an ODE flow s.t. $\mathbf{x}\sim p_0$ implies \(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x})\sim p_t\).
        • We may choose a flow map s.t.
          • \(\displaystyle\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}) := \mu(t) + \sigma(t)\left( rac{\mathbf{x}-\mu(0)}{\sigma(0)} ight)\).
        • Since the flow map should be invertible, we have
          • \( orall\mathbf{y} = \Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}),\quad\exists\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}:\mathbb{R}^D ightarrow\mathbb{R}^D ext{ s.t. }\mathbf{x} = \Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}(\mathbf{y}) = \Psi_{t ightarrow 0}(\mathbf{y})\).
      3. Deriving the velocity field.
        • \(\displaystyle\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}) = rac{\sigma'(t)}{\sigma(t)}(\mathbf{x}-\mu(t)) + \mu'(t)\).
          • i.e.) \(\mathbf{v}_t = \partial_t \Psi_{0 ightarrow t} \circ \Psi^{-1}_{0 ightarrow t}\).
          • Why?)
            • Putting $\mathbf{x}_0$ be the initial input, we may get
              • \(\displaystyle\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mu(t) + \sigma(t)\left( rac{\mathbf{x}_0-\mu(0)}{\sigma(0)} ight)\quad\cdots\quad(A)\).
              • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t} (\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}_0)) = \mathbf{v}_t (\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}_0))\quad\cdots\quad(B)\).
            • For some $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$,
              • we may rewrite (A) as
                \( egin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_0 &= \Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})&\cdots\quad(C1)&\quad( ecause ext{Invertibility}) \ &= \mu(0) + \sigma(0)\left( rac{\hat{\mathbf{x}}-\mu(t)}{\sigma(t)} ight) &\cdots\quad(C2) \end{aligned}\).
                • which is equivalent to \(\displaystyle rac{\mathbf{x}_0-\mu(0)}{\sigma(0)} = rac{\hat{\mathbf{x}}-\mu(t)}{\sigma(t)}\quad\cdots(C3)\)
              • we may rewrite (B) as
                • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t} (\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}_0)) = \mathbf{v}_t (\hat{\mathbf{x}})\quad\cdots\quad(D)\).
            • Plugging (C1) into (D), we may get
              • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t}\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})) = \mathbf{v}_t(\hat{\mathbf{x}})\quad\cdots\quad(E)\).
            • From our definition, we may also get
              • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t}\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}) = \mu'(t) + \sigma'(t)\left( rac{\mathbf{x}-\mu(0)}{\sigma(0)} ight)\) given $\mathbf{x}\quad\cdots(F)$
            • Replacing \(\mathbf{x}\) in (F) with \(\mathbf{x}_0 = \Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})\) from (C1,2), we get \( egin{aligned} rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t}\Psi_{0 ightarrow t} igg(\overbrace{\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})}^{=\mathbf{x}_0} igg) &= \mu'(t) + \sigma'(t)\Big( rac{\overbrace{\mathbf{x}_0}^{=\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}^{-1}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})}-\mu(0)}{\sigma(0)}\Big) & ( ext{From } (C1,2)) \ &= \mu'(t) + \sigma'(t)\Big( rac{\hat{\mathbf{x}}-\mu(t)}{\sigma(t)}\Big) & ( ecause (C3)) \ &= rac{\sigma'(t)}{\sigma(t)}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}-\mu(t)) + \mu'(t) \ &= \mathbf{v}_t(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) & ( ecause (E)) \end{aligned}\).
            • Again replacing $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ with $\mathbf{x}$ we get
              • \(\displaystyle\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}) = rac{\sigma'(t)}{\sigma(t)}(\mathbf{x}-\mu(t)) + \mu'(t)\).
      4. Showing the closed-form in marginal also works in conditional.
        • Two strategies for choosing the $\mathbf{z}$ from $p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$
          1. Two-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = (\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)\)
            • Settings)
              • \(\mathbf{z}\sim \pi(\mathbf{z}) := p_{ ext{src}}(\mathbf{x}_0) p_{ ext{tgt}}(\mathbf{x}_1)\).
              • \(p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z}=(\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_1)) := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t;\; a_t \mathbf{x}_0 + b_t\mathbf{x}_1, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})\).
                • where $a_t+b_t = 1$, i.e. the linear interpolation between \(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1\)
            • Deriving $\mathbf{v}_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$
              • Then applying the derivation of $\mathbf{v}_t$ in marginal, we may get
                • \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{z}) = a_t' \mathbf{x}_0 + b_t' \mathbf{x}_1\).
            • CFM Loss
              • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{CFM}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{ ext{src}}, \mathbf{x}_1\sim p_{ ext{tgt}}}\left\Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - (a_t' \mathbf{x}_0 + b_t' \mathbf{x}_1) ight\Vert^2\).
          2. One-Sided Conditioning : Consider the case of \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_1\)
            • Settings)
              • \(\mathbf{z}\sim \pi(\mathbf{z}) := p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}_1)\).
              • \(p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z}=\mathbf{x}_1) := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t;\; b_t \mathbf{x}_1, a_t^2\mathbf{I})\).
                • where \( egin{cases} a_0 = 1, b_0 = 0 \ a_1 = 0, b_1 = 1 \ \end{cases} \Leftrightarrow egin{cases} p_0(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z}=\mathbf{x}_1) = \mathcal{N}(\cdot;\; \mathbf{0,I}) \ p_1(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z}=\mathbf{x}_1) = delta(\cdot - \mathbf{x}_1) \ a_1 = 0, b_1 = 1 \ \end{cases}\)
            • Deriving $\mathbf{v}_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$
              • Then applying the derivation of $\mathbf{v}_t$ in marginal, we may get
                • \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}_1) = b_t'\mathbf{x}_1 + rac{a_t'}{a_t}(\mathbf{x} - b_t\mathbf{x}_1)\).
            • CFM Loss
              • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{CFM}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{ ext{src}}, \mathbf{x}_1\sim p_{ ext{tgt}}}\left\Vert \mathbf{v}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_t, t) - \left[ b_t'\mathbf{x}_1 + rac{a_t'}{a_t}(\mathbf{x} - b_t\mathbf{x}_1) ight] ight\Vert^2\).


Concept) Lagrangian View

  • Goal)
    • Design the conditional flow map $\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\cdot;\mathbf{z})$ first and directly obtain the corresponding $\mathbf{v}_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})$ by differentiating along particle trajectories, i.e. \( rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t}\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{y};\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{v}_t\Big(\underbrace{\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{y};\mathbf{z})}_{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{z}\Big)\)
  • e.g.) Conditional Affine Flow
    • Settings)
      • \(\mathbf{z}\sim\pi\) : a latent variable
        • which can be (but not limited to) either…
          1. Two-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = (\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)\)
          2. One-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_0\) or \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_1\)
        • Refer to Eulerian View for more details.
      • \(\Psi_{0 ightarrow t}(\mathbf{x}_0;\mathbf{z}) := \mu_t(\mathbf{z}) + \mathbf{A}_t(\mathbf{z})\mathbf{x}_0,\quad t\in[0,1]\) : the time-varying conditional affine flow
        • where
          • \(\mu_t(\mathbf{z})\in\mathbb{R}^D\).
            • cf.) \(\mu_0(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{0}\)
          • \(\mathbf{A}_t(\mathbf{z})\in\mathbb{R}^{D imes D}\) is invertible for \(t\in[0,1]\)
            • cf.) \(\mathbf{A}_0(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{I}\)
    • Induce the Conditional Path \(p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})\) :
      • \(p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z}) = [\Psi_t(\cdot;\mathbf{z})]_\# p_0\).
        • cf.) \(\#\) means pushing the density forward which is equivalent to the change-of-variables
          • i.e.) \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t) = p_0(\Psi_t^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_t)) \left\vert \det abla_{\mathbf{x}_t} \Psi_t^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_t) ight\vert\)
        • Prop.)
          • Since $\mathbf{A}$ is affine, if $p_0$ is Gaussian, then $p_t$ is Gaussian as well.
      • \(p_t(\cdot) = \displaystyle\int p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})\pi(\mathbf{z}) ext{d}\mathbf{z}\).
    • Derive the Conditional Velocity \(\mathbf{v}_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{z})\) :
      • Applying the derivation result \(\displaystyle\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}) = rac{\sigma'(t)}{\sigma(t)}(\mathbf{x}-\mu(t)) + \mu'(t)\) from the Eulerian View, we may get
        • \(\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{z}) = \mu_t'(\mathbf{z}) + \mathbf{A}_t'(\mathbf{z})\mathbf{A}_t(\mathbf{z})^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_t(\mathbf{z}))\).
    • Depending on the conditioning strategy…
      1. One-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_0\) or \(\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}_1\)
        • We may set $\Psi_t$ as
          \( egin{cases} \mu_t(\mathbf{z}) = b_t\mathbf{z} \ \mathbf{A}_t(\mathbf{z}) = a_t \mathbf{I} \ \end{cases},\quad egin{cases} a_0 = 1, b_0 = 0 \ a_1 = 0, b_1 = 1 \ \end{cases}\).
        • Then, we have
          • \(\mathbf{x}_t = a_t\mathbf{x}_0 + b_t,\quad \mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}_1) = b_t'\mathbf{x}_1 + rac{a_t'}{a_t}(\mathbf{x} - b_t \mathbf{x}_1)\).
        • Thus,
          • \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_1) = a_t'\mathbf{x}_0 + b_t'\mathbf{x}_1\).
      2. Two-Sided Conditioning : \(\mathbf{z} = (\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)\)
        • We may set $\Psi_t$ as
          \( egin{cases} \mu_t(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1) = b_t\mathbf{x}_1 \ \mathbf{A}_t(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1) = a_t \mathbf{I} \ \end{cases}\).
        • Then, we have
          • \(\mathbf{x}_t = a_t\mathbf{x}_0 + b_t\mathbf{x}_1,\quad p_t(\cdot\mid\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1) = \delta(\cdot - (a_t\mathbf{x}_0 + b_t\mathbf{x}_1))\).
        • Thus,
          • \(\mathbf{v}_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_1) = a_t'\mathbf{x}_0 + b_t'\mathbf{x}_1\).

Model) Rectified Flow

  • Refer to the previous note on RF

entry.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.4 Score SDE Famework
entry.content:
Diffusion Model Conceptual Reconstruction following The Principles of Diffusion Models


Hozy Summary

  • Training Score SDE
    • Forward-Time SDE : \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t),) ext{d}t + g(t) ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\)
    • Loss : \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\omega(\cdot)) := \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{t}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0}\mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0})}\left[ \omega(t) \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) - abla_{\mathbf{x}_{t}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0}) \Vert_2^2 ight]\).
  • Sampling Score SDE
    • Reverse-Time SDE : \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{SDE}}(t) = \Big[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{SDE}}(t), t) - g^2(t) \underbrace{s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{SDE}}(t), t)}_{ ext{plugged in}} \Big] ext{d}t + g(t) ext{d} ar{\mathbf{w}}(t)\)
      • Sampling Scheme : \(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t} \;\leftarrow\; \mathbf{x}_{t} - \left[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) - g^2(t)s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}, t) ight]\Delta t + g(t)\sqrt{\Delta t} \cdot oldsymbol{\epsilon}\)
    • PF-ODE : \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t} \mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(t), t) - g^2(t) \underbrace{s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(t), t)}_{ ext{plugged in}}\)
      • Sampling Scheme : \(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t} \;\leftarrow\; \mathbf{x}_{t} - \left[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) - g^2(t)s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}, t) ight]\Delta t\)
  • Instantiations of SDE
    • VE SDE
      • e.g.) NCSN
    • VP SDE
      • e.g.) DDPM



4.1 Score SDE: Its Principles

Concept) Forward-Time SDE : Data to Noise

  • Def.)
    • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t),t) ext{d}t + g(t) ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
      • where
        • $t\in[0,T]$ : a continuous timestep
        • $\mathbf{f}(\cdot, t) : \mathbb{R}^D ightarrow\mathbb{R}^D$ : the drift
        • $g(t)\in\mathbb{R}$ : the scalar diffusion coefficient
  • Prop.)
    • We may induce time-dependent densities of
      • \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t\mid\mathbf{x}_0)\) : Perturbation Kernel
      • \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)\) : Marginal Density


Analysis) Deriving Forward SDE from NCSN & DDPM's Common Form
NCSN DDPM
Forward Noise Injection Schemes $$\mathbf{x}_{\sigma_i} = \mathbf{x} + \sigma_i\epsilon_i$$ $$\mathbf{x}_i = \sqrt{1- eta_i^2}\mathbf{x}_{i-1} + eta_i\epsilon_i$$
Discrete Sequential Update $$t ightarrow t+\Delta t$$ $$ egin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_{t+\Delta t} &= \mathbf{x}_t + \sqrt{\sigma_{t+\Delta t}^2 - \sigma_t^2}\epsilon_t \ &\approx \mathbf{x}_t + \underbrace{\sqrt{ rac{ ext{d}\sigma_t^2}{ ext{d}t}}}_{ ext{diffusion}}\epsilon_t\sqrt{\Delta t} \end{aligned}$$ $$ egin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_{t+\Delta t} &= \sqrt{1- eta_t}\mathbf{x}_t + \sqrt{ eta_{t}}\epsilon_t \ &\approx \mathbf{x}_t \underbrace{- rac{1}{2} eta_t\mathbf{x}_t}{ ext{drift}}\Delta t + \underbrace{\sqrt{ eta_t}}_{ ext{diffusion}}\epsilon_t\sqrt{\Delta t} \end{aligned}$$
Common Form $$ ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = \underbrace{\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t),t) ext{d}t}_{ ext{drift}} + \underbrace{g(t)}_{ ext{diffusion}} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)$$
Corresponding Gaussian transition is $$p(\mathbf{x}_{t+\Delta t}\mid\mathbf{x}_t) := \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{x}_{t+\Delta t};\; \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_t, t)\Delta t, g^2(t)\Delta t\mathbf{I} ight)$$ $$ ext{where } egin{cases} \mathbf{x}(t+\Delta t) - \mathbf{x}(t) & \approx ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) \ \Delta t &\approx ext{d}t \ \sqrt{\Delta t} \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},, \Delta t\mathbf{I}) &\approx ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t) \end{cases}$$
$$ ext{where d}\mathbf{w}(t)\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, ext{d}t\mathbf{I}) ext{ is a standard Wiener process}$$



Concept) Reverse-Time Stochastic Process for Generation

  • Def.)
    • \( ext{d} ar{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \left[ \mathbf{f}( ar{\mathbf{x}}(t), t) - g^2(t) abla_\mathbf{x}\log p_t( ar{\mathbf{x}}(t)) ight] ext{d}t + g(t) ext{d} ar{\mathbf{w}}(t)\).
      • where
        • \( ar{\mathbf{x}}(T)\sim p_{ ext{prior}} \approx p_T\).
        • \( ar{\mathbf{w}}(t)\) denotes a standard Wiener process in reverse time s.t.
          • \( ar{\mathbf{w}}(t) := \mathbf{w}(T-t) - \mathbf{w}(T)\).
  • Prop.)
    • Balance between the drift and the score-driven drift
      • drift : \(g(t) ext{d} ar{\mathbf{w}}(t)\)
        • This term
          • provides controlled stochasticity for exploration.
          • dominates at the early stage : $t\approx T$
      • score=-driven drift : \(g^2(t) abla_\mathbf{x}\log p_t( ar{\mathbf{x}}(t))\)
        • This term
          • guides trajectories toward regions of higher density
          • dominates at the later stage : $t\approx 0$



Concept) Probability Flow ODE (PF-ODE)

  • Goal)
    • A deterministic process (ODE) that evolves samples with the same marginal distributions as the forward SDE.
  • Def.)
    • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t} ilde{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}( ilde{\mathbf{x}}(t), t) - rac{1}{2} g^2(t) abla_\mathbf{x} \log p_t( ilde{\mathbf{x}}(t))\).
  • Sample result)
    • \( ilde{\mathbf{x}}(T) + \displaystyle\int_T^0 \left[ \mathbf{f}( ilde{\mathbf{x}( au)}, au) - rac{1}{2}g^2( au) abla_\mathbf{x}\log p_ au( ilde{\mathbf{x}}( au)) ight] ext{d} au\quad\) where $ ilde{\mathbf{x}}(T)\sim p_{ ext{prior}}$
      • Intractable
      • relies on numerical solvers
        • e.g.) Euler Method
  • Advantages over Reverse-Time SDE
    • Bidirectionalilty of Integration
      • i.e.) Can be integrated in either direction : $\int_0^T$ or $\int_T^0$
    • Wide range of well-established numerical solvers for ODE.
  • Prop.)
    • Derived by choosing a drift of an ODE s.t. its evolution preserves the same marginal densities as the forward SDE


Prop.)

  • Fokker-Plank Equation ensures that Reverse SDE and PF-ODE yield identical marginal distribution \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)\) given the Forward SDE.


E.g.) Gaussian Closed Form Example

  • Settings)
    • $p_{x_t}\sim\mathcal{N}(m_t, s_t^2)$
Reverse-Time SDE Derivation

Recall that the Forward SDE was given by $$ ext{d}x(t) = f(t)x(t) ext{d}t + g(t) ext{d}w_t$$ If we take one small Euler step of size $\Delta t\gt0$, we have $$ egin{aligned} x_{t+\Delta t} = ax_t + r\epsilon \quad ext{where } egin{cases} a := 1+f(t)+\Delta t \ r := g(t)\sqrt{\Delta t} \ \epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ Equivalently, the forward one-step transition kernel is Gaussian of $$x_{t+\Delta t}\vert x_t\sim\mathcal{N}(a x_t, r^2)$$ Also, the current marginal at $t$ is also Gaussian because $p_{ ext{data}}$ is Gaussian, i.e. $$x_t\sim\mathcal{N}(m_t, s_t^2)\quad\exists m_t, s_t\in\mathbb{R}$$ By Bayes' rule, we may denote the conditional density as $$ egin{aligned} p(x_t\mid x_{t+\Delta t}) &\varpropto p(x_{t+\Delta t}\mid x_t) \; p_t(x_t) \ &\varpropto \exp\left(- rac{(x_{t+\Delta t} - ax_t)^2}{2r^2} ight) \exp\left(- rac{(x_t-m_t)^2}{2s_t^2} ight) \end{aligned}$$ Taking the log on both sides, we may simplify into $$ egin{aligned} \log p(x_t\mid x_{t+\Delta t}) &= - rac{(x_{t+\Delta t} - ax_t)^2}{2r^2} - rac{(x_t-m_t)^2}{2s_t^2} + C \ &=-\left( rac{a^2}{2r^2}+ rac{1}{2s_t^2} ight)x_t^2 -\left( rac{-2ax_{t+\Delta t}}{2r^2} + rac{-2m_t}{2s_t^2} ight) x_t + C' \ &=- rac{A}{2}x_t^2 + B x_t + C'' & \left( ext{Put } A = rac{a^2}{r^2}+ rac{1}{s_t^2},\; B= rac{ax_{t+\Delta t}}{r^2} + rac{m_t}{s_t^2} ight) \ &= - rac{A}{2}\left(x_t- rac{B}{A} ight)^2 - rac{B^2}{A} + C'' \ \end{aligned}$$ Considering that for $x\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2),\;\log x = - rac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}+C$, we may get $$ ext{Var}(x_t\mid x_{t+\Delta t}) = rac{1}{A} = rac{1}{ rac{a^2}{r^2}+ rac{1}{s_t^2}},\quad \mathbb{E}[x_t\mid x_{t+\Delta t}] = rac{B}{A} = rac{ rac{ax_{t+\Delta t}}{r^2} + rac{m_t}{s_t^2}}{ rac{a^2}{r^2}+ rac{1}{s_t^2}}$$

PF-ODE Derivation

Refer to p102-104
Idea : Denote $\Delta t$ as a smooth map as $$x_{t+\Delta t} = \Phi_{t,\Delta t}(x_t) = x_t + \Delta t v_t(x_t) + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2)$$ Goal is to see what form $v_t$ must take so that output is Gaussian given an Gaussian input.



4.2 Training and Sampling of Score SDE

Tech.) Training Score SDE

  • Loss : DSM Loss
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\omega(\cdot)) := \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{t}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0}\mathbb{E}_{p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0})}\left[ \omega(t) \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) - abla_{\mathbf{x}_{t}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0}) \Vert_2^2 ight]\).
      • where \(\mathbf{x}_{0}\sim p_{ ext{data}}\)
        • cf.) This is the continuous counterpart of the NCSN Loss of
          • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{NCSN}}(\phi) := \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^L \lambda(\sigma_i) \mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\sigma_i)\).
        • Prop.)
          • The minimizer $s^*$ satisfies
            \( egin{aligned} s^*(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_{0}\sim p(\mathbf{x}_{0}\mid\mathbf{x}_{t})} \left[ abla_{\mathbf{x}_{t}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0}) ight] \ &= abla_{\mathbf{x}_{t}}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}) \quad\quad ext{for almost every } \mathbf{x}_{t}\sim p_t ext{ and } t\in[0,T] \end{aligned}\)

Tech.) Sampling with Reverse-Time SDE

  • Setting)
    • \(s_{\phi^ imes} := s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}, t) \approx abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})\).
      • Trained and frozen parameterized score
  • Sampling)
    • Plugging in the $s_{\phi^ imes}$ into the Reverse-Time SDE, we may get
      • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{SDE}}(t) = \Big[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{SDE}}(t), t) - g^2(t) \underbrace{s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{SDE}}(t), t)}_{ ext{plugged in}} \Big] ext{d}t + g(t) ext{d} ar{\mathbf{w}}(t)\).
    • Draw an initial value $\mathbf{x}T$ from $p{ ext{prior}}$.
    • From $t=T$ to $t=0$, solve the above equation using a numerical solver.
      • e.g.) Euler-Maruyama Method
        • \(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t} \;\leftarrow\; \mathbf{x}_{t} - \left[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) - g^2(t)s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}, t) ight]\Delta t + g(t)\sqrt{\Delta t} \cdot oldsymbol{\epsilon}\).
          • where
            • \( oldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0, I})\),
            • $\Delta t\gt0$ is the step size.
  • Prop.)
    • DDPM’s sampling scheme is one specification with some choice of $\mathbf{f}$ and g.


Tech.) Sampling with PF-ODE

  • Setting)
    • \(s_{\phi^ imes} := s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}, t) \approx abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x})\).
      • Trained and frozen parameterized score
  • Sampling)
    • Plugging in the $s_{\phi^ imes}$ into the PF-ODE, we may get
      • \(\displaystyle rac{ ext{d}}{ ext{d}t} \mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(t), t) - g^2(t) \underbrace{s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(t), t)}_{ ext{plugged in}}\).
    • Draw an initial value $\mathbf{x}T$ from $p{ ext{prior}}$.
    • From $t=T$ to $t=0$, solve the above equation
      • This is equivalent to approximating the below integral:
        • \(\underbrace{\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}(0)}_{ ext{final sample}} \;=\; \mathbf{x}(T) + \displaystyle\int_T^0 \left[ \mathbf{f} \left(\mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}( au), au ight) - rac{1}{2}g^2( au)s_{\phi^ imes}\left( \mathbf{x}_{\phi^ imes}^{ ext{ODE}}( au) ight) ight] ext{d} au\).
      • Or, if we use the Euler method, we may update in discrete timestep $\Delta t$ as
        • \(\mathbf{x}_{t-\Delta t} \;\leftarrow\; \mathbf{x}_{t} - \left[ \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{t}, t) - g^2(t)s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}, t) ight]\Delta t\).
          • cf.) Completely deterministic!
  • Application)
    • Controllable Generation
      • e.g.) Mokady et al., 2023; Su et al., 2022
      • Idea)
        • If we solve the PF-ODE in forward direction, ODE flow maps data to its latent representation.
  • Prop.)
    • Exact log-likelihood computation via PF-ODE
Desc.



4.3 Instantiations of SDEs

Concept) Variance Explosion SDE (VE SDE)

  • Def.)
    • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = \displaystyle\sqrt{ rac{ ext{d}\sigma^2(t)}{ ext{d}t}} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
      • i.e.)
        • Drift Term : $\mathbf{f} = 0$
        • Diffusion Term : \(g(t) = rac{ ext{d}\sigma^2(t)}{ ext{d}t}\)
  • Props.)
    • Perturbation Kernel : \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0}) = \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t};\; \mathbf{x}_{0}, \left( \sigma^2(t) - \sigma^2(0) ight)\mathbf{I} ight)\)
    • Prior Distribution : \(p_{ ext{data}} := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2(T)\mathbf{I})\)
      • where $\sigma(t)$ is an increasing function for $t\in[0,T]$ and that $\sigma^2(T)\gg\sigma^2(0)$
  • e.g.)
    • NCSN with \(\displaystyle\sigma(t) := \sigma_\min\left( rac{\sigma_\max}{\sigma_\min} ight)^t,\quad ext{for } t\in(0,1]\)
      • i.e.) Discretized version of VE SDE


Concept) Variance Preserving SDE (VP SDE)

  • Def.)
    • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = \displaystyle - rac{1}{2} eta(t)\mathbf{x}(t) ext{d}(t) + \sqrt{ eta(t)} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
      • i.e.)
        • Drift Term : $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t), t) = - rac{1}{2} eta(t)\mathbf{x}(t)$
        • Diffusion Term : \(g(t) = \sqrt{ eta(t)}\)
  • Props.)
    • Perturbation Kernel : \(p_t(\mathbf{x}_{t}\mid\mathbf{x}_{0}) = \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t};\; \mathbf{x}_0 \exp\left(- rac{1}{2}\int_0^t eta( au) ext{d} au ight), \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{I}\exp(-\int_0^t eta( au) ext{d} au) ight)\)
    • Prior Distribution : \(p_{ ext{data}} := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0, I})\)
  • e.g.)
    • DDPM with noise schedule of \( eta(t) := eta_\min + t( eta_\max - eta_\min),\quad orall t\in[0,1]\)
      • i.e.) Discretized version of VP SDE

entry.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.3 Score-Based Perspective - From EBMs to NCSN

link.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.3 Score-Based Perspective - From EBMs to NCSN

entry.content:
Diffusion Model Conceptual Reconstruction following The Principles of Diffusion Models


Hozy Summary

EBM $ ightarrow$ Score Matching $ ightarrow$ Score-Based Generative Model $ ightarrow$ Sliced Score Matching / Denoising Score Matching $ ightarrow$ NCSN

  • EBM
    • Def.)
      • a model that assigns low energy (stability) on the probable datapoint.
      • \(\displaystyle p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) := rac{\exp{(-E_\phi(\mathbf{x}))}}{Z_\phi}\).
    • Drawback)
      • Intractable $Z_\phi$ and its gradient.
  • Score Matching
    • Def.)
      • Calculate the score function which is the gradient of the log-density
        • \(s(\mathbf{x}) := abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x}),\quad s:\mathbb{R}^D ightarrow\mathbb{R}^D\).
    • Why it works?
      • \( abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p(\mathbf{x}) = abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log ilde{p}(\mathbf{x}) - \underbrace{ abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log Z}_{=0} = abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log ilde{p}(\mathbf{x})\).
        • where
          • $ ilde{p}(\mathbf{x})$ : an unnormalized density
            • e.g.) $\exp(-E_\phi(\mathbf{x}))$ in EBMs.
          • \(p(\mathbf{x}) = rac{ ilde{p}(\mathbf{x})}{Z},\quad Z = \int ilde{p}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x}\).
    • Advantage)
      • Tractability : $Z_\phi$ is now gone!
      • Complete Representation of the Underlying distribution.
    • Drawback)
      • Needs 2nd order derivative, which is computationally prohibitive in high dimensions.
    • Sampling Method)
      • Langevin SDE
        • Def.)
          • Discrete
            • \(\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_{n} + \eta abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{n}) + \sqrt{2\eta}\epsilon_n\),
              • for \(\eta\gt0, n=0,1,\ldots, \epsilon_n\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0, I})\)
          • Continuous ($\eta ightarrow0$)
            • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}(t)) ext{d}t + \sqrt{2} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
        • Advantage)
          • Solving simple Langevin SDE is equivalent to sampling from the estimated distribution.
            • Why?) Under standard regularity assumptions, the distribution of $\mathbf{x}(t)$ converges to $p_\phi$ as $t ightarrow\infty$.
        • Limit)
          • Sensitivity to hyperparmeters
          • Poor Mixing Time
  • Score-Based Generative Model
    • Idea)
      • Let neural network to parameterize the score function directly
        • \(s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \approx s(\mathbf{x})\).
    • Loss)
      \( egin{aligned} ilde{\mathcal{L}}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) &:= \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}} \left[ \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) - s(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight] \ &\equiv \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}} \left[ ext{Tr}\left( abla_\mathbf{x} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight) + rac{1}{2} \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight] \ \end{aligned}\).
    • Sampling)
      • Discrete
        • \(\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_{n} + \eta s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{n}) + \sqrt{2\eta}\epsilon_n,\quad \epsilon_n\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})\).
      • Continuous
        • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}(t)) ext{d}t + \sqrt{2} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
    • Drawback)
      • $O(D^2)$ cost for training
        • cf.) \( ext{Tr}\left( abla_\mathbf{x} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight)\) from \( ilde{\mathcal{L}}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi)\)
    • Improvements)
      • Sliced Score Matching
        • \( ilde{\mathcal{L}}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x,u}} \left[ \mathbf{u}^ op( abla_\mathbf{x} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}))\mathbf{u} + rac{1}{2}(\mathbf{u}^ op s_\phi(\mathbf{x}))^2 ight]\).
          • i.e.) Replaced the trace of the Jacobian with VJP operation in automatic differentiation
          • Still the reliance on the raw data makes the training fragile
      • Denoising Score Matching
        • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi, \sigma) := \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}},\; ilde{\mathbf{x}}\sim p_\sigma(\cdot\mid\mathbf{x})} \left[ \Vert s_\phi( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) - abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight]\).
          • i.e.) Perturb the data with noise with fixed scale of $\sigma$ and condition on the data.
          • e.g.) Additive Gaussian Noise
            • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi, \sigma) = rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, \epsilon} \left[ \left\Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}+\sigma\epsilon;\sigma) + rac{\epsilon}{\sigma} ight\Vert_2^2 ight]\).
        • By Tweedie’s Formula, DSM is equivalent to denoising.
        • Limit)
          • Single fixed noise $\sigma$ cannot capture the dynamics on the noise level.
          • Langevin dynamics can be slow to converge or even fail in high-dimensional spaces.
  • Noise Conditional Score Network (NCSN)
    • Idea)
      • Inject Gaussian noise at multiple levels into the data distribution
      • Jointly train to estimate score functions across a range of noise scales.
    • Loss)
      • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{NCSN}}(\phi) := \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^L \lambda(\sigma_i) \mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\sigma_i)\),
        • where
          • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\sigma_i) = rac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}} \left[ \left\Vert s_\phi( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) - rac{\mathbf{x}- ilde{\mathbf{x}}}{\sigma^2} ight\Vert_2^2 ight]\).
          • $\lambda(\sigma_i)\gt0$ : a weighting function for each scale
    • Sampling)
      • Annealed Langevin dynamics



3.1 Energy Based Models (EBMs)

Def.) Energy Function

  • Settings)
    • \(\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^D\) : a data point
  • Def.)
    • \(E_\phi(\mathbf{x})\) : an energy function parameterized by \(\phi\) s.t.
      • assigns lower energy to more likely configureations
      • \(\displaystyle p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) := rac{\exp{(-E_\phi(\mathbf{x}))}}{Z_\phi}\).
      • \(\displaystyle Z_\phi := \int_{\mathbb{R}^D}\exp(-E_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})) ext{d}\mathbf{x}\) : the partition funciton ensuring normalization.
        • i.e.) \(\displaystyle\int_{\mathbb{R}^D} \displaystyle p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} = 1\)
  • Prop.)
    • Lower energy corresponds to higher probability.
      • In our setting \( rac{\partial }{\partial E_\phi(\mathbf{x})} \exp{(-E_\phi(\mathbf{x}))} \lt 0\)
      • More stable state.
    • As the model trains (well),
      • it assigns high(low) probabilities on more(less) probable datapoints as the image below.
      • the probability mass is redistributed across the entire space rather than assigned independently to each region.
  • Training)
    • MLE (log)
      • Intractable
        • \(\log Z_\phi\) and its gradient
        • Alternative Sols.)
          • Contrastive divergence
          • Score matching


Concept) Score

  • Settings)
    • \(p(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbb{R}^D ightarrow [0,\infty)\) : a density
  • Def.)
    • The score function is the gradient of the log-density
      • \(s(\mathbf{x}) := abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x}),\quad s:\mathbb{R}^D ightarrow\mathbb{R}^D\).
  • Prop.)
    • The score forms a vector field that points toward regions of higher probability.
  • Advantage)
    • Tractability
      • Why?) The partial function $Z$ cancels out.
        • For
          • $ ilde{p}(\mathbf{x})$ : an unnormalized density
            • e.g.) $\exp(-E_\phi(\mathbf{x}))$ in EBMs.
          • \(p(\mathbf{x}) = rac{ ilde{p}(\mathbf{x})}{Z},\quad Z = \int ilde{p}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x}\),
        • \( abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p(\mathbf{x}) = abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log ilde{p}(\mathbf{x}) - \underbrace{ abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log Z}_{=0} = abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log ilde{p}(\mathbf{x})\).
    • Complete Representation of the Underlying distribution
      • \(\log p(\mathbf{x}) = \log p(\mathbf{x}_0) + \displaystyle\int_0^1 s(\mathbf{x}_0 + t(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0))^ op (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_0) ext{d}t\).
        • where
          • $\mathbf{x}_0$ is a reference point
          • $\log p(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is fixed by normalization
        • cf.)
          \( egin{aligned} \int_0^1 s(\mathbf{x}_0 + t(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0))^ op (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_0) ext{d}t &= \int_0^1 s(r(t))^ op (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_0) ext{d}t & ( ext{Put } r(t) = \mathbf{x}_0 + t(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0)) \end{aligned}\) : interpolation


Model) Score Matching

Hyvärinen and Dayan, 2005

  • Idea)
    • Match the data and model scores!
  • Loss
    \( egin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) &= rac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{p_{ ext{data}(\mathbf{x})}} \left\Vert abla_\mathbf{x}\log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) - abla_\mathbf{x}\log p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) ight\Vert_2^2 \ &= \mathbb{E}_{p_{ ext{data}(\mathbf{x})}} \left[ ext{Tr}\left( abla_\mathbf{x}^2 E_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight) + rac{1}{2} \Vert abla_\mathbf{x} E_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight] + C \ \end{aligned}\).
    • where
      • \( abla_\mathbf{x}^2 E_\phi(\mathbf{x})\) is the Hessian of $E_\phi$
      • $C$ is a constant independent of $\phi$.
    • Advantage)
      • $Z$ is gone!
      • Avoids sampling from the model during training.
    • Drawback)
      • Needs 2nd order derivative, which is computationally prohibitive in high dimensions.


Concept) Langevin Sampling

Concept) Discrete-Time Langevin Dynamics

  • Def.)
    \( egin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_{n+1} &= \mathbf{x}_{n} - \eta abla_{\mathbf{x}} E_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{n}) + \sqrt{2\eta}\epsilon_n & n=0,1,\ldots \ &= \mathbf{x}_{n} + \eta abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{n}) + \sqrt{2\eta}\epsilon_n & ( ecause ext{By def. of the score}) \end{aligned}\).
    • where
      • $\mathbf{x}_{0}$ is initialized from some distribution.
      • $\eta\gt0$ : the step size
      • $\epsilon_n\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})$ : Gaussian noise
  • Prop.)
    • $\epsilon_n$ enables exploration beyond local minima by adding stochasticity.
    • The score function $\log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{n})$ guides the samples toward high-density regions.

Concept) Langevin Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE)

  • Idea)
    • $\eta ightarrow0$ on Discrete-Time Langevin Dynamics.
  • Def.)
    • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_\phi(\mathbf{x}(t)) ext{d}t + \sqrt{2} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
  • Prop.)
    • Discrete-time update rule serves as the Euler-Maruyama discretization of this continuous SDE.
    • Under standard regularity assumptions, the distribution of $\mathbf{x}(t)$ converges to $p_\phi$ as $t ightarrow\infty$.
      • Thus, we may sample by simply solving this SDE.
  • Limit)
    • In high-dimensional spaces,
      • its efficiency is highly sensitive to…
        • step size $\eta$
        • noise scale
        • number of iterations
      • it has the issue of poor mixing time.
        • Desc.)
          • Requires extremely long time to transition between regions of high probability
          • The more the data distribution is mixed with many isolated modes, the longer it takes.
            • Why?) Recall that it relies on the local stochastic updates to escape the local minima
              • \(\sqrt{2} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).



3.2 Score-Based Generative Model

Tech.) Parameterizing Score Function with Neural Network

  • Ideation)
    • Recall that the score \(s(\mathbf{x})= abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_ ext{data}(\mathbf{x})\) is enough to reconstruct $p_{ ext{data}}$.
    • Then, why don’t we train our model to directly parameterize the score?
      • i.e.) \(s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \approx s(\mathbf{x})\)
    • We may set up a naive MSE loss as below.
      • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) := \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}} \left[ \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) - s(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight]\).
        • However, we don’t know \(s(\mathbf{x})\).
    • Hyvärinen and Dayan (2005) suggested an equivalent objective that depends only on $s_\phi(\mathbf{x})$.
  • Props.)
    • When we solve the \( ilde{\mathcal{L}}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi)\) minimization problem…
      • the divergence term \( ext{Tr}\left( abla_\mathbf{x} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight)\) favors negative value
      • the norm term \( rac{1}{2} \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2\) suppresses the score $s_\phi$
        • Thus, the result stationary points act as a attractive sinks.
          • i.e.) The points where the dynamics terminates!
          • p66 for details on stationarity and sink.
  • Sampling
    • Same Langevin dynamics can be used for sampling plugging in the frozen oracle score as…
      • Discrete
        • \(\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_{n} + \eta s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{n}) + \sqrt{2\eta}\epsilon_n,\quad \epsilon_n\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})\).
          • cf.) \(\eta s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}_{n})\) is updated in the direction that increases the density. (Opposite direction of the energy $E_\phi$ case.)
      • Continuous
        • \( ext{d}\mathbf{x}(t) = s_{\phi^ imes}(\mathbf{x}(t)) ext{d}t + \sqrt{2} ext{d}\mathbf{w}(t)\).
  • Drawback)
    • Training is still too costly.
      • Why?)
        • Calculating \( ext{Tr}\left( abla_\mathbf{x} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) ight)\) takes $O(D^2)$ times.



Model) Sliced Score Matching

Song et al. 2020

  • Idea)
    • Simplify the loss by adopting the isotropic random vector and the Hutchinson’s identity $\mathbf{u}\in\mathbb{R}^D$
  • Model)
    • For a isotropic random vector $\mathbf{u}\in\mathbb{R}^D$
    • \( ilde{\mathcal{L}}_{ ext{SM}} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x,u}} \left[ \mathbf{u}^ op( abla_\mathbf{x} s_\phi(\mathbf{x}))\mathbf{u} + rac{1}{2}(\mathbf{u}^ op s_\phi(\mathbf{x}))^2 ight]\).
  • Advantage)
    • Cheaper computation
      • No more Jacobian nor Hessian
      • Simply utilize the automatic differentiation
        • Jacobian- and vector-Jacobian-product operations (JVP/VJP)
          • Meaning)
            • Check the model’s behavior along random directions : the projected score is nudged to align with regions of higher data density, so data points become stationary in expectation.
  • Limit)
    • Its reliance on the raw data distribution makes the training fragile.
      • Why?)
        • for image data lying on low-dimensional manifolds, the score \( abla_\mathbf{x} \log p_ ext{data}(\mathbf{x})\) may be undefined or unstable
        • the method only constrains the vector field at observed points, providing weak control in their neighborhoods


Concept) Isotropic Random Vector & Hutchinson’s Identity

  • Def.)
    • a random vector whose statistical properties are uniform in all directions
    • i.e.) \(\mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbf{u} ight] = \mathbf{0},\quad \mathbb{E} igg[\underbrace{ \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^ op }_{ ext{outer prod}} igg] = \mathbf{I}\)
  • Hutchinson’s Identity)
    • \( ext{Tr}(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{u}}\left[ \mathbf{u^ op A u} ight]\).
    • \(\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{u}}\left[(\mathbf{u}^ op \mathbf{v})^2 ight] = \Vert \mathbf{v}\Vert_2^2\).
Pf. $$ egin{aligned} (1) &\; ext{Tr}(\mathbf{A}) = ext{Tr}(\mathbf{AI}) = ext{Tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{uu^ op}]) = ext{Tr}(\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{u^ op A u}]) \ (2) &\; \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{u^ op v})^2]=\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{(u^ op v)^ op (u^ op v)}] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{(v^ op u)(u^ op v)}] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{v^ op v}] = \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 \ \end{aligned}$$




3.3 Denoising Score Matching

Vincent, 2011

  • Idea)
    • Recall we had the originally parameterized loss of \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi) := \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}} \left[ \Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}) - abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_ ext{data}(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight]\).
    • Here, the score \( abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_ ext{data}(\mathbf{x})\) was intractable.
    • What if we replace $p_{ ext{data}}$ with marginally perturbed distribution \(p_{\sigma}( ilde{\mathbf{x}})\)?
      • \(p_{\sigma}( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) = \displaystyle\int p_{\sigma}( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x}\).
        • i.e.) Injecting noise into the data $\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}$ via a known conditional distribution \(p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})\) with scale $\sigma$
    • Although, \( abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})\) is intractable
      • Vincent showed that conditioning on $\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}}$ yields an equivalent tractable objective : Denoising Score Matching (DSM)
  • Model)
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi, \sigma) := \displaystyle rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{ ext{data}},\; ilde{\mathbf{x}}\sim p_\sigma(\cdot\mid\mathbf{x})} \left[ \Vert s_\phi( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) - abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^2 ight]\).
  • Props.)
    • The optimal minimizer $s^*( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma)$ satisfies
      • \(s^*( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) = abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})\).
    • \(\sigma ightarrow0\Rightarrow egin{cases} p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ightarrow p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \ s^*( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) = abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ightarrow abla_{\mathbf{x}}\log p_ ext{data}(\mathbf{x}) \end{cases}\).
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}\) is equivalent to \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{SM}}\).
      • i.e.) \(\exists C\in\mathbb{R} ext{ s.t. } \mathcal{L}_{ ext{SM}}(\phi;\sigma) = \mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\sigma) + C\)
    • Denoising Score Matching is equivalent to denoising.
      • \(\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}]}_{ ext{denoising}} = \displaystyle rac{1}{\alpha} \underbrace{\left( ilde{\mathbf{x}} + \sigma^2 abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} \log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ight)}_{\approx ext{DSM Langevin Sampling}}\).
        • Refer to Tweedie’s Formula below.
  • Sampling
    • Langevin dynamics
      • \( ilde{\mathbf{x}}_{n+1} = ilde{\mathbf{x}}_{n} + \eta \underbrace{s_{\phi^ imes}( ilde{\mathbf{x}}_{n})}_{\approx abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}_n)} + \sqrt{2\eta}\epsilon_n,\quad \epsilon_n\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})\).
  • Advantage)
    • Well-defined gradients
      • Injected noise provides full support in $\mathbb{R}^D$.
        • cf.) Recall that data point is a low-dimensional manifold.
      • Thus, the score function is well defined everywhere.
    • Improved coverage.
      • Injected noise smooths out sparse regions between modes.
      • This enhances the training signal quality and facilitates the sampling.
  • Limit)
    • Single fixed noise $\sigma$ cannot capture the dynamics on the noise level.
      • Low Noise Level
        • Langevin dynamics struggles to traverse modes in multi-modal distributions due to vanishing gradients in low-density regions.
      • High Noise Level
        • The model captures only coarse structures, resulting in blurry samples.
    • Langevin dynamics can be slow to converge or even fail in high-dimensional spaces.


E.g.) Additive Gaussian Noise DSM

  • Model)
    • Perturbation : $ ilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon,\quad \epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0, I})$
    • Then
      • \(p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\; \mathbf{x}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})\).
      • \( abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \displaystyle rac{\mathbf{x}- ilde{\mathbf{x}}}{\sigma^2}\).
    • Thus, we may further simplify the loss as…
      \( egin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi, \sigma) &= rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}} \left[ \left\Vert s_\phi( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) - rac{\mathbf{x}- ilde{\mathbf{x}}}{\sigma^2} ight\Vert_2^2 ight] \ &= rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, \epsilon} \left[ \left\Vert s_\phi(\mathbf{x}+\sigma\epsilon;\sigma) + rac{\epsilon}{\sigma} ight\Vert_2^2 ight] \ \end{aligned}\).


Concept) Tweedie’s Formula

  • Goal)
    • We want to show that DSM is equivalent to denoising.
  • Settings)
    • Denoising
      • We may define denoising as $\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}$.
        • i.e.) getting the clean data $\mathbf{x}$ given the noisy data $ ilde{\mathbf{x}}$.
      • The expected value of such values can be a reasonable estimate
        • \(\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p(\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}})}[\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}]\).
  • Result)
    • \(\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}]}_{ ext{denoising}} = \displaystyle rac{1}{\alpha} \underbrace{\left( ilde{\mathbf{x}} + \sigma^2 abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} \log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ight)}_{\approx ext{DSM Langevin Sampling}}\).
  • Meaning)
    • Optimizing the DSM score and Langevin sampling from it is a perfect denoiser.
Derivation. - Assume that the perturbation kernel (or conditional distribution of $ ilde{\mathbf{x}}$ given $\mathbf{x}$) is Gaussian. $$\displaystyle p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\; \alpha\mathbf{x}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I}) \varpropto \exp\left(- rac{\Vert ilde{\mathbf{x}} - \alpha\mathbf{x}\Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} ight)$$ --- cf.) Other exponential family distribution works thanks to the deferentiation rule below.
- Then, we may get the gradient of the prior distribution as $$\displaystyle abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}}\; p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid \mathbf{x}) = - rac{ ilde{\mathbf{x}} - \alpha\mathbf{x}}{\sigma^2}\;p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) \quad\cdots\quad (A)$$ - Now, consider that DSM score can be rewritten as $$ egin{aligned} abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} \log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) &= rac{1}{p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})} abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) \ &= rac{1}{p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})} abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} \left( \int p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} ight) \ &= rac{1}{p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})} \int abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} \ &= \int rac{1}{p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})} \underbrace{ abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x})}_{(A)} p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} \ &= -\int rac{ ilde{\mathbf{x}} - \alpha\mathbf{x}}{\sigma^2}\; rac{p( ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}) p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}})} ext{d}\mathbf{x} \ &= -\int rac{ ilde{\mathbf{x}} - \alpha\mathbf{x}}{\sigma^2}\; p(\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} & ( ecause ext{Bayes' Rule}) \ &= - rac{1}{\sigma^2} \left( ilde{\mathbf{x}} \int p(\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} - \alpha \int \mathbf{x} p(\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ext{d}\mathbf{x} ight) \ &= - rac{1}{\sigma^2} \left( ilde{\mathbf{x}} - \alpha \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}] ight) \ \end{aligned}$$ - Above can be rewritten as $$\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mid ilde{\mathbf{x}}] = rac{1}{\alpha} \left( ilde{\mathbf{x}} + \sigma^2 abla_{ ilde{\mathbf{x}}} \log p_\sigma( ilde{\mathbf{x}}) ight)$$



3.4 Multi-Noise Levels of Denoising Score Matching

Model) Noise Conditional Score Networks (NCSN)

Song et al 2019

  • Idea)
    • Recall that DSM’s single fixed noise scale $\sigma$ was a bottleneck.
    • Instead,
      • inject Gaussian noise at multiple levels into the data distribution
      • jointly train a noise-conditional score network (NCSN) to estimate score functions across a range of noise scales.
  • Training)
    • Settings)
      • \(0\lt\sigma_{1}\lt\sigma_{2}\lt\cdots\lt\sigma_{L}\) : a sequence of $L$ noise levels
        • $\sigma_{1}$ is small enough to preserve most of the data’s fine details
        • $\sigma_{L}$ is large enough to sufficiently smooth the distribution, fascilitating easier training.
      • Perturbation Kernel : \(p_\sigma(\mathbf{x}_\sigma\mid\mathbf{x}) := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_\sigma;\; \mathbf{x}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I}\)
      • Marginal Distribution : \(p_\sigma(\mathbf{x}_\sigma) = \displaystyle\int p_\sigma(\mathbf{x}_\sigma\mid\mathbf{x}) p_{ ext{data}}(\mathbf{x}) ext{d}\mathbf{x}\)
    • Loss
      • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{NCSN}}(\phi) := \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^L \lambda(\sigma_i) \mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\sigma_i)\),
        • where
          • \(\mathcal{L}_{ ext{DSM}}(\phi;\sigma_i) = rac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, ilde{\mathbf{x}}\mid\mathbf{x}} \left[ \left\Vert s_\phi( ilde{\mathbf{x}};\sigma) - rac{\mathbf{x}- ilde{\mathbf{x}}}{\sigma^2} ight\Vert_2^2 ight]\).
          • $\lambda(\sigma_i)\gt0$ : a weighting function for each scale
    • Result)
      • \(s^*(\mathbf{x}_\sigma, \sigma) = abla_{\mathbf{x}_\sigma}\log p_\sigma(\mathbf{x}_\sigma)\).
        • i.e.) Optimal score for each noise level $\sigma_i$
      • Prop.)
        • By Tweedie’s Formula, this is equivalent to the Bayes optimal noise predictor under DDPM loss.
          • Refer to x-prediction.
  • Sampling)
    • Annealed Langevin Dynamics
      • cf.) Step Size
        • Typically scaled by the noise level as
          • \(\eta_\ell = \delta\cdot rac{\sigma_\ell^2}{\sigma_1^2}\).
  • Prop.)
    • Slow Sampling speed.
      • $O(LK)$ where $L$ is the number of noise scales and $K$ is the iterative step in each scale during the annealed Langevin Dynamics.



Analysis) NCSN vs DDPM

entry.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.2 Variational Perspective - From VAEs to DDPM

link.title: (DM Reconst.) Ch.2 Variational Perspective - From VAEs to DDPM

entry.content:
Diffusion Model Conceptual Reconstruction following The Principles of Diffusion Models


Hozy Summary

  • VAE (Naive Autoencoder $\rightarrow$ VAE $\rightarrow$ Gaussian VAE) cannot resolve the blurriness issue due to the averaging effect.
  • HVAE enables more expressive power with the progressive structure (deep, layered hierachical network).
    • Still, HVAE causes the posterior collapse problem if the layers get too deep.
      • i.e. Loss of control in generation.
  • DDPM
    • resembles…
      • the encoder-decoder structure of the VAEs, with forward–reveres structure
      • the deep layered structure of HVAE employing the timestep $t$
    • but
      • has the fixed encoder (not the target of learning)
      • has no per-level KL terms
        • instead well conditioned denoising subproblems from large to small noise
      • yields stable optimization
      • has high sample quality while preserving a coarse-to-fine hierarcy over time (noise)
    • yet
      • has slow sampling speed for accuracy



2.1 Variational Autoencoder (VAE)

  • hozy review on Kingma et al.


Model) Naive Autoencoder

  • Structure)
    • Original Data $\rightarrow$ Encoder $\rightarrow$ Latent $\rightarrow$ Decoder $\rightarrow$ Reconstructed Data
  • Training)
    • Minimzie the reconstruction error between original input and reconsturction.
  • Drawback)
    • Unstructured latent space
      • i.e.) Latent codes produce meaningless outputs.
      • Sol.) VAE


Model) Variational Autoencoder

Kingma et al. 2013

  • Structure)
    • Decoder (Generator)
      • Settings)
        • $\mathbf{x}$ : observed variable
        • $\mathbf{z}\sim p_{\text{prior}}$ : latent variable
          • where $p_{\text{prior}} := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})$
      • Goal)
        • Map $\mathbf{z}$ back to data space.
        • Sample $\mathbf{z}\sim p_{\text{prior}}$ and decode into $\mathbf{x}\sim p_\phi(\mathbf{x\mid z})$.
      • Def.)
        • $p_\phi(\mathbf{x\mid z})$ : a decoder distribution
      • Marginal likelihood)
        • $p_\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \displaystyle\int p_\phi(\mathbf{x\mid z}) p(\mathbf{z}) \text{d}\mathbf{z}$
          • Problem)
            • Integral over $\mathbf{z}$ is intractable!
            • Sol.) Variational step
    • Encoder (Inference Network)
      • Goal)
        • Given an observation $\mathbf{x}$, map to the latent code $\mathbf{z}$.
          • i.e.) $p_\phi(\mathbf{z\mid x})$
      • Problem)
        • $p_\phi(\mathbf{z\mid x})$ is intractable.
          • Why?)
            • $p_\phi(\mathbf{z\mid x}) = \displaystyle\frac{p_\phi(\mathbf{x\mid z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{p_\phi(\mathbf{x})}\quad(\because\text{Bayes’ Rule})$
            • $p_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ was intractable.
        • Sol.) Variational step.
  • Tech.)
    • Variational Step
      • Goal.)
        • Find a encoder $q_\theta(\mathbf{z\mid x})$ s.t. $q_\theta(\mathbf{z\mid x}) \approx p_\phi(\mathbf{z\mid x})$
        • How?)
          • Parameterize with a neural network.
      • Optimization)
        • Minimize the ELBO loss.


Concept) Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

  • Props.)
    • Reconstruction Term
      • Encourages accurate recovery of $\mathbf{x}$ from the latent $\mathbf{z}$.
      • With Gaussian VAE guarantees the reconstruction loss of an autoencoder.
      • (RISK) Optimizing this alone has risk of memorizing the training data!
    • Latent Regularization (KL)
      • Encourages $q_\theta(\mathbf{z\mid x})$ (the encoder distribution) to stay close to $p_{\text{prior}}$ (the simple prior).
      • Shapes the latent space into a smooth and continuous structure.



Model) Gaussian VAE

  • Goal)
    • Employ Gaussian distributions for both the encoder and decoder.
  • Def.)
    • Encoder : $q_\theta(\mathbf{z\mid x}) := \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{z};\;\mu_\theta(\mathbf{x}), \text{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2_\theta(\mathbf{x})\right)\right)$
      • where
        • $\mu_\theta(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbb{R}^D\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^d$
        • \(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2_\theta : \mathbb{R}^D\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^d_+\).
    • Decoder : $p_\phi(\mathbf{z\mid x}) := \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x};\;\mu_\phi(\mathbf{z}), \sigma^2\mathbf{I} \right)$
      • where
        • $\mu_\phi(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbb{R}^d\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^D$
        • Fixed variance with $\sigma\gt0$
  • Optimization) ELBO loss
  • Drawback)
    • Blurry Generation
      • Why?)
        • Averaging effect over conflicting modes
Desc of the averaging effect



Model) Hierarchical VAE (HVAE)

Vahdat et al., 2020

  • Structure)
    • Decoding
      • Forms chain of conditional priors
      • Top-down Hierarchy of multiple layers of latent variables.
        • Top-down factorization of the joint distributions
          • \(\displaystyle p_\phi(\mathbf{x, z}_{1:L}) = p_\phi(\mathbf{x, z}_{1}) \prod_{i=2}^L p_\phi(\mathbf{z}_{i-1}\mid\mathbf{z}_{i}) p(\mathbf{z}_{L})\).
      • Marginal data distribution
        • \(p_{\text{HVAE}}(\mathbf{x}) := \displaystyle\int p_\phi(\mathbf{x, z}_{1:L}) \text{d}\mathbf{z}_{1:L}\).
    • Encoding)
      • Bottom-up Markov Factorization
        • \(q_\theta(\mathbf{z}_{1:L}\mid\mathbf{x}) = q_\theta(\mathbf{z}_{1}\mid\mathbf{x}) \prod_{i=2}^L q_\theta(\mathbf{z}_i\mid\mathbf{z}_{i-2})\).
  • Optimization)
    • ELBO loss
Derivation

  • Desc.)
    • Distributed information penalty across levels and localizes learning signals through the adjacent KL terms.


  • Advantages)
    • Captures data features at multiple levels of abstraction.
    • Boosts expressive power
    • Mirrors the compositional nauure of real-world data
    • Utilizing the deep, stacked layers to build data
      • Deep networks!
  • Limits)
    • Single Gaussian variational family cannot match the multimodal $p_\phi(\mathbf{z\mid x})$.
    • If the decoder is too expressive, the model may suffer from posterior collapse.
      • i.e.) When decoder can model the data with out using $\mathbf{z}$, we lose control of the generation.
Desc. using the mutual information derived from the ELBO loss




2.2 Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)

Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020

  • hozy review on Ho et al.


Concept) Forward Process (Fixed Encoder)

  • Goal)
    • Gradually corrupt data by injecting Guassian noise over multiple steps via a transition kernel of
      \(\begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{x}_{i}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i-1}) &:= \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{x}_i;\; \sqrt{1-\beta_i^2} \mathbf{x}_{i-1},\; \beta_i^2\mathbf{I} \right) \\ &\triangleq \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{x}_i;\; \alpha_i \mathbf{x}_{i-1},\; (1-\alpha_i^2)\mathbf{I} \right) & \left( \alpha_i \stackrel{\text{put}}{=} 1-\beta_i^2 \right) \\ \end{aligned}\).
      • where
        • \(\mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{\text{data}}\) : a sample drawn from the real data distribution
        • \(\left\{ \beta_i \right\}_{i=1}^L\) : a pre-determined monotoncially increasing noise schedule for \(\beta_i\in(0,1), \forall i\)
      • which is equivalent to updating $\mathbf{x}_i$ as
        • \(\mathbf{x}_i = \alpha_i \mathbf{x}_{i-1} + \beta_i \epsilon_i\).
          • for \(\epsilon_i\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0, I})\)
  • Desc.)
    • Closed-Form Expression for the distribution of noisy samples at step $i$
      • \(p(\mathbf{x}_{i}\mid \mathbf{x}_{0}) = \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{x}_i;\; \bar{\alpha}_i\mathbf{x}_0, (1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2)\mathbf{I} \right)\).
        • where \(\bar{\alpha} := \displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^i\sqrt{1-\beta_k^2} = \prod_{k=1}^k \alpha_k\)
    • Sampled $\mathbf{x}_i$
      • \(\bar{\alpha}_i\mathbf{x}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2}\epsilon\).
        • for \(\epsilon_i\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0, I})\)
    • Convergence of the forward process
      • If \(\left\{ \beta_i \right\}_{i=1}^L\) is an increasing sequence
        • \(p_L(\mathbf{x}_L\mid\mathbf{x}_0)\rightarrow\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,1})\) as $L\rightarrow\infty$
      • Justifying $p_{\text{prior}} := \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})$


Concept) Reverse Process (Learnable Decoder)

  • Goal)
    • Reverse the noise corruption through a parameterized distribution \(p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i})\)
  • Method)
    • To get the tractable marginal, we condition the intermediate noisy data on the clean data sample.
      • Refer to the DDPM optimization problem below.
    • Then we may get the closed form reverse conditional transition kernel of
  • Parameterization)
    • \(p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid\mathbf{x}_i) := \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{x}_{i-1};\; \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_i, i), \sigma^2(i)\mathbf{I} \right)\).
      • where
        • \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\cdot, i) : \mathbb{R}^D\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^D\) : a learnable mean function
        • $\sigma^2(i)$ : a fixed variance function given from the reverse conditional transition kernel above.


Tech) Optimization of DDPM

  • Goal)
    • Minimize the expected KL divergence of
      • \(\mathbf{E}_{p_i(\mathbf{x}_i)}\left[ \mathcal{D}_{\text{KL}}\left( p(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i}) \Vert p_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i}) \right) \right]\).
  • Problem)
    • \(p(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i})\) is intractable.
      • Why?)
        • \(p_i(\mathbf{x}_i), p_{i-1}(\mathbf{x}_{i-1})\) are intractable where \(p(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i}) = p(\mathbf{x}_{i}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i-1}) \displaystyle\frac{p_{i-1}(\mathbf{x}_{i-1})}{p_i(\mathbf{x}_i)}\)
        • We don’t know $p_{\text{data}}$ so as \(p_i(\mathbf{x}_i)=\displaystyle\int p_i(\mathbf{x}_i\mid\mathbf{x}_0) p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}_0)\text{d}\mathbf{x}_0\)
  • Sol.)
    • Condition the reverse transition on a clean data sample $\mathbf{x}$
      • i.e.) \(p(\mathbf{x}_{i-1}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i}, \mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}_{i}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i-1}) \displaystyle\frac{p_{i-1}(\mathbf{x}_{i-1} \mid \mathbf{x})}{p_i(\mathbf{x}_i \mid \mathbf{x})}\).
      • How does this work?
        1. Markov forward process assumption enables \(p(\mathbf{x}_{i}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i-1}, \mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}_{i}\mid \mathbf{x}_{i-1})\)
        2. Gaussian assumption on all involved distributions.


Tech.) DDPM’s Loss Function

  • Def.)
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{DDPM}}(\phi) := \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^L\frac{1}{2\sigma^2(i)}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0}\mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{x}_i\mid\mathbf{x}_0)}\left[ \left\Vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i) - \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_0, i) \right\Vert_2^2 \right]\).
Derivation of the Loss


  • Parameterizations of \(\mu_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i)\)
    • $\epsilon$-prediction
    • $\mathbf{x}$-prediction
      • cf.) Both are mathematically equivalent.
        • While the true forward process uses ground-truth $\mathbf{x}_0$ and real noise $\epsilon$, our predictions must obey this exact same structural identity:
          \(\begin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_i = \bar{\alpha}_i\mathbf{x}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_i, i) + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2}\epsilon_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i) &\Leftrightarrow \mathbf{x}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_i, i) = \frac{1}{\bar{\alpha}_i} \left( \mathbf{x}_i - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2}\epsilon_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i) \right) \\ &\Leftrightarrow \epsilon_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2}} \left( \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\alpha}_i\mathbf{x}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_i, i) \right) \\ \end{aligned}\)
  • ELBO


Tech.) ε-prediction

  • Goal)
    • Train a neural network to estimate the noise added.
  • Def.)
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{i}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{\text{data}}}\mathbb{E}_{\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})} \left[ \Vert \epsilon_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i) - \epsilon \Vert_2^2 \right]\).
      • where \(\mathbf{x}_i = \bar{\alpha}_i\mathbf{x}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} \epsilon\) with \(\mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{\text{data}}\).
Derivation of the Loss


  • Prop.)
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{DDPM}}\) and \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}}\) share the same optimal solution of
      • \(\epsilon^*(\mathbf{x}_i, i) = \mathbb{E}[\epsilon\mid\mathbf{x}_i]\) for \(\mathbf{x}_i\sim p_i\)
        • i.e.) Noise estimated by the ε-prediction coincides with the conditional expectation of the true noise, even though $\mathbf{x}_i$ does not uniquely determine the original clean sample.


Tech.) x-prediction

  • Goal)
    • Train a neural network to estimate the clean image from a given noisy input \(\mathbf{x}_i\sim p_i(\mathbf{x}_i)\).
  • Def.)
    • \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{x-pred}}(\phi) := \mathbb{E}_{i}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0 \sim p_{\text{data}}}\mathbb{E}_{\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0,I})} \left[ \omega_i \Vert \mathbf{x}_\phi(\mathbf{x}_i, i) - \mathbf{x}_0 \Vert_2^2 \right]\).
      • where
        • \(\mathbf{x}_0\sim p_{\text{data}}\).
